cards.front: Name the 3 layers of Cisco's hierarchical campus design and what each does.
cards.back: Access: connects end devices (PCs, APs, printers); high port density. Distribution: aggregates access switches, enforces policy (ACLs, QoS), L3 boundary. Core: high-speed backbone between distribution blocks. Each layer has clear responsibilities — keeps the network scalable and predictable.

cards.front: What is collapsed core, and when is it appropriate?
cards.back: Combine distribution and core into one tier (no separate core layer). Suitable for small/medium campuses where a separate core would be over-engineering. Most modern enterprise designs are 2-tier (collapsed) — keeps cabling and L3 boundaries simpler.

cards.front: What is the spine-leaf topology, and where is it used?
cards.back: Data center fabric. Every leaf (top-of-rack switch) connects to every spine; no leaf-to-leaf or spine-to-spine links. Equal cost paths between any two leaves (always 2 hops). Optimized for east-west (server-to-server) traffic, which dominates modern apps.

cards.front: What is a SOHO LAN, and what device commonly handles it?
cards.back: Small Office / Home Office — small network with usually one wireless router/firewall combining router + L2 switch + Wi-Fi AP + DHCP + NAT in one device. Cisco/Meraki MR/MX appliances or consumer-grade routers (Netgear, ASUS).

3-tier (Access + Distribution + Core): scales to large campuses with many distribution blocks. 2-tier (Access + Distribution-as-Core): cheaper, simpler, suitable for small-medium sites. Choice depends on size, scaling needs, traffic patterns.

cards.front: What is PoE, and what are the IEEE standards?
cards.back: Power over Ethernet — delivers DC power over the same UTP cable as data. 802.3af (PoE): 15.4W. 802.3at (PoE+): 30W. 802.3bt Type 3: 60W. 802.3bt Type 4 (UPoE): 90W. Used to power phones, APs, cameras, IoT — eliminates separate power.

cards.front: Network topology terms: star, full mesh, partial mesh.
cards.back: Star: all devices connect to one central hub/switch (typical LAN). Full mesh: every device connects to every other device (n × (n-1) / 2 links — used for redundancy in DC core or DMVPN spokes). Partial mesh: some pairs of devices have direct links but not all (compromise — common in WAN designs).

cards.front: Spine-leaf rules — what connects to what?
cards.back: Every LEAF (top-of-rack) connects to every SPINE. Leaf must NOT connect to leaf; spine must NOT connect to spine. End hosts connect ONLY to leaves. All paths between any two leaves are EXACTLY 2 hops (leaf → spine → leaf), enabling predictable latency and ECMP. Optimized for east-west (server-to-server) traffic in modern data centers.
